title: 2 2 Lagrange Multipliers
body:
2.2 LAGRANGE MULTIPLIERS

The method of Lagrange multipliers

To find the local minima and maxima of f (x, y) with the constraint g(x, y) = 0 we find the values of x, y and λ that simultaneously satisfy the equations

∂f ∂g ∂f ∂g −λ = 0, −λ = 0, together with g(x, y) = 0 ∂x ∂x ∂y ∂y

In real-world applications, we are not usually interested in just finding the maximum or minimum value of a quantity. Often there are additional constraints that must be satisfied. For example the question ‘Find the dimensions of the closed circular cylinder of smallest surface area’ is not really a sensible question. Much more interesting is the problem ‘Find the dimensions of the closed circular cylinder of smallest surface area whose volume is 16π cm3 . ’

A geometrical view of the problem To formulate the problem, we first need to express our side condition in the generic form g(x, y) = 0. We then look for minimum and maximum values of f (x, y), such that the points (x, y) satisfy g(x, y) = 0.

Problems of this kind can be solved using the Method of Lagrange Multipliers. We will provide a more intuitive proof in the next lecture, but we can understand the method as follows:

Form the Lagrangian function L(x, y, λ) = f (x, y) − λg(x, y). We refer to λ as a Lagrange multiplier, a dummy variable that is introduced to make sure that the constraint is satisfied. In that case, g(x, y) = 0 and L(x, y, λ) and f (x, y) are the same function with the same extreme values.

What this means is that, rather than looking for extreme values of f (x, y) subject to the constraint g(x, y) = 0, we can instead look for extreme values of L(x, y, λ) with no constraint. This is done in the usual manner. The only change is that we are now dealing with a function of three variables.

∂L ∂f ∂g ∂f ∂g =0→ −λ =0→ =λ ∂x ∂x ∂x ∂x ∂x

∂L ∂f ∂g ∂f ∂g =0→ −λ =0→ =λ ∂y ∂y ∂y ∂y ∂y

∂L = 0 → −g(x, y) = 0 → g(x, y) = 0 ∂λ

Notice that the last condition gives us our constraint back!

Solving these equations will generate a basket of points for consideration. We DO NOT test for max/min as in the previous lecture but rather simply check for max/min by direct evaluation into f (x, y).

Example 1 Find the extreme values of z = f (x, y) = 12 + 3x + 4y with the constraint

x2 + y 2 − 1 = 0

Note that the question ‘Find the extreme values of z = f (x, y)’ makes no sense here! The surface z = f (x, y) is a plane, which has no maximum or minimum value. The constraint is essential!

Let’s take a careful look at the problem geometrically.

Figure 1: Example 1: f (x, y) = 12 + 3x + 4y subject to the constraint x2 + y 2 − 1 = 0.

F Maximum of 17 at ( 53 , 45 ). Minimum of 7 at (− 35 , − 54 ) F

Observe that we DO NOT run the second derivative test here. Instead, we simply calculate which points gives the largest and smallest values of f (x, y).

Example 2 Find the minimum value of z = f (x, y) = x4 + y 4 subject to the condition

x+y−1=0

Figure 2: Example 2: f (x, y) = x4 + y 4 subject to the constraint x + y − 1 = 0.

F Minimum of at ( 12 , 21 ) F How do we know this point is a minimum and not a maximum?

Example 3 Find the dimensions of the closed circular cylinder of smallest surface area whose volume is 16π cm3 .

Surface area = S = 2πr2 + 2πrh.

Volume= πr2 h = 16π.

Hence the Lagrange question is to minimise

S = 2πr2 + 2πrh

subject to the constraint that

g(r, h) = πr2 h − 16π = 0

∂S ∂g =λ → 4πr + 2πh = λ2πrh → 2r + h = λrh. (I) ∂r ∂r

∂S ∂g =λ → 2πr = λπr2 → 2 = λr. (II) (Note r 6= 0) ∂h ∂h

We also have:

g(r, h) = 0 → πr2 h − 16π = 0 → r2 h = 16. (III) 2 2 (II) → λ = . Substituting in (I) yields 2r + h = rh → 2r + h = 2h → 2r = h. r r Finally we sub into (III) to get r2 (2r) = 16 → r3 = 8 → r = 2 → h = 4.

Note that we know this yields the minimum surface area since there is clearly no maximum surface area. That is we can make the cylinder as large as we wish and still keep the volume 16π cubic centimetres!

F r = 2 cm, h = 4 cm F

Example 4 Use Lagrange multipliers to find the minimum distance between the plane 3x + 4y + 12z = 6 and the point (5, 7, 11).

Let P (x, y, z) be an arbitrary p point in space. The distance from the fixed point (5, 7, 11) to P (x, y, z) is then given by (x − 5)2 + (y − 7)2 + (z − 11)2 . So the problem we need to solve is: p • Minimize (x − 5)2 + (y − 7)2 + (z − 11)2 subject to the constraint g(x, y, z) = 3x + 4y + 12z − 6 = 0.

Notice that we now have an extra dimension but the method of Lagrange Multipliers still works except that we will now have 4 equations in 4 unknowns x, y, z and λ !

Before jumping in, we will first simplify our calculations by dropping the square root and dealing instead with the equivalent problem:

• Minimise (x − 5)2 + (y − 7)2 + (z − 11)2 subject to the constraint that g(x, y, z) = 3x + 4y + 12z − 6 = 0.

F shortest distance of 13 at the point on the plane (2, 3, −1) F

How do we know that this is the minimum distance and not a maximum? You can now do Q 39 to 47
